chunks.text: All right, friends, Greg Kokel here, and I just realized that wow, it's getting into close to, within striking distance of reality season. Now, karma is the idea that, I guess in common parlance, what goes around comes around, and that was reflected a little bit there in the responses of the people that you talked to, Kristen, and that if you're doing bad things, then bad things happen to you, and you're doing good things, then good things happen to you. Now, I used to live in Thailand, and it was quite a while ago. It's a long time ago. And in Thai, they had a phrase called tampon. Tampon. Tam means to do or to make, and bon was their word for good, okay? So when you tampon, you would be doing good, or the way that the phrase actually was applied was you're making merit. So you could go in Bangkok, for example, and there'll be vendors in the street, and there'll be a vendor that had a little cage with a sparrow in it. So this cage has the sparrow. The sparrow's caged up. And you could buy the cage from the vendor and then open the door and release the bird. And because you're releasing the bird, this is an act of tampon, of making merit. And so merit accrues to your karmic account. So you've done a good thing, released the bird, and now that's karmic money in the bank, so to speak. Now, it's, oh, that's so sweet, you know, but when you think about it kind of worldviewishly, there are other questions about whether that whole worldview is sound, but even in a more of a micro about the karma thing, wait a minute, the only way I could do good to the bird to tampon, to make merit on behalf of my karmic debt is to release the bird. Well, how'd the bird get in the cage? The bird got in the cage because somebody captured the bird and put it in the cage which means he's not tampon, he's tampon. He's bad. He's making bad. I don't know if that's the phrase they use, but what about him? Now he's accruing against himself the karmic debt for capturing the bird in the first place, and you just gave money to him. So how does this all work in the kind of calculus of karma? I don't know. But it does seem odd to me, and I don't even know why it is that someone would believe that that's the way the world is structured because it also creates some other kinds of odd situations. So the person who said that, oh, you did something good. Well, good things. We're good people and good things happen to good people. Okay, is that really true? And the same thing is true, LDS friends. And the principle here is the second step of the tactical game plan, and that is the burden of proof issue. The person who makes the claim bears the burden. If you make a claim, good, that's a start, but you got to take it further. It's not my job to refute your claim, it's your job to support it, to justify it. Okay, so that's the first thing. A couple of other things come to mind here. As if the early church had authority in itself, and then something happened, they disappeared. And this does raise the question of the gates of hell shall not prevail. Well, maybe it prevailed, what, for 1800 years before Joseph Smith? I don't know. How would you answer that, LDS missionary, whoever? I may ask a fair question, right? And then all of a sudden, now you have a new group of people who are vested with the same power the old group of people had. And what I'm saying is that it was not the case that the group of people, any group of people had that power. The authority always came from the text. Those who were disciples of the disciples of Jesus did not claim that authority for themselves. They went back to the text. And this is where you could read J. Warner Wallace's wonderful book, Cold Case Christianity, because he talks about the nature of the message and how it's been handed down over time from one to the other. And he uses a cop illustration, and he calls it the chain of custody. So if there's a crime that's committed and there's evidence of the crime, they label it, and then they mark it with the date. And some one person gives it to another person. He labels it as having received it. And then they give it to another person who labels it as having. And so the chain of custody follows this piece of evidence so that when it finally comes to trial, that evidence can be displayed as being the bona fide evidence from the beginning because it went through this chain of custody. Fair enough? It's a great illustration. I love the analogy with his police work, but it's a great characterization of what took place because the message, the content, the details were not guarded by a group of people who were the church that guarded it, the organization that guarded it. It was guarded by the passing on of the writings of the apostles. And when you see the writings of the earliest Christian leaders, they were all quoting the same texts and saying the same thing. So you have a de facto chain of custody down through the years, not just from John, but from Peter, etc., etc. And Jay Warner quantifies this really superbly by quoting the church fathers and seeing what they say. The gospel wasn't lost. First of all, contrary to the claims of the LDS church, and there was no church authority that was guarding it as a church, which then passes that authority somehow mystically down 2,000 years to this other group of people who now has the same authority.
